findings.why: make_token uses random.choice (non-cryptographic PRNG) to generate session and password-reset tokens, making them predictable.
build_where interpolates user-supplied keyword and status directly into SQL string passed to raw_query, enabling SQL injection.
create_order passes user-supplied callback_url directly to fetch() with no validation of scheme or destination, enabling SSRF.
verify_tls defaults to false (VERIFY_TLS env var must be explicitly set to 'true'), so outbound TLS certificate verification is off by default.
fetch() passes settings.verify_tls to httpx.Client, which defaults to False per config, disabling TLS verification on all outbound requests.
Admin routes /admin/orders and /admin/orders/{id}/status have no authentication or authorization check; any unauthenticated caller can access or modify all orders.
get_order fetches any order by ID without verifying the requesting user owns it, allowing access to other users' orders.
update_order patches any order by ID without verifying the requesting user owns it, allowing modification of other users' orders.
update_order builds UPDATE SET from all non-null fields in OrderUpdate including user_id and amount_cents, letting callers reassign order ownership or manipulate pricing.
OrderUpdate exposes user_id and amount_cents as patchable fields, allowing clients to change order ownership and financial amounts.
update_order builds SET clause by interpolating column names from model fields directly into SQL string without whitelisting, enabling SQL injection via crafted field names.
confirm_reset compares token hashes via SQL equality which may short-circuit, and no constant-time comparison is used when validating the reset token.
confirm_reset rate-limits on a global key 'reset:confirm' rather than per-token or per-user, allowing distributed brute-force of reset tokens across different keys.
change_password and confirm_reset do not call revoke_all_for_user after credential change, violating the documented invariant and leaving existing sessions valid after a takeover.
on_error logs request.url.path unsanitized via log.exception, allowing attackers to inject newlines and fake log entries through crafted URL paths.
make_token uses random.choice (non-CSPRNG) to generate session and reset tokens, making them predictable
admin router has no authentication or authorization check; any caller can list all orders or change any order status
build_where interpolates user-supplied keyword and status directly into SQL string passed to raw_query, enabling SQL injection
update_order builds SET clause by interpolating column names from model_dump keys directly into an f-string SQL query without allowlist validation
create_order fetches user-supplied callback_url with no scheme allowlist, IP validation, or SSRF protection
verify_tls defaults to False, so all outbound HTTPS requests skip certificate verification unless the env var is explicitly set
fetch passes settings.verify_tls to httpx.Client, which is False by default, disabling TLS certificate verification
get_order fetches any order by ID without checking that the order belongs to the authenticated user
update_order patches any order by ID without verifying ownership by the authenticated user
OrderUpdate exposes status, amount_cents, and user_id as client-writable fields, allowing privilege escalation and price/owner manipulation
update_order applies all non-null fields from OrderUpdate including user_id and amount_cents directly to the DB without server-side filtering
user_for looks up sessions by raw token equality in SQL rather than using a constant-time comparison, enabling timing-based token oracle
confirm_reset rate-limits on a global key reset:confirm rather than per-token or per-user, allowing distributed brute-force of reset tokens
DATABASE_URL defaults to a hardcoded plaintext localhost connection string in source code
request.url.path is logged directly without sanitizing CR/LF characters, enabling log injection via crafted URLs
change_password does not revoke existing sessions after a password change, allowing stolen sessions to remain valid indefinitely
confirm_reset does not revoke all existing user sessions after a successful password reset, leaving compromised sessions active
